ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for spicy salmon maki; mapped to the closest parent class 'sushi roll food product' (FOODON_03311069). Spicy salmon maki is a specific variant with raw salmon and chili-seasoned dressing.
ontology.foodex2_description: Sushi roll (maki) — rice-based Japanese roll with fish filling wrapped in nori
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers sushi/maki rolls broadly; no dedicated code for spicy salmon maki variant. Mapped to closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for spicy salmon maki or sushi roll food product as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of spicy salmon maki is approximately 150 g, equivalent to one full roll of 6–8 pieces, as commonly served in Japanese restaurants and sushi bars.

nutrient_source.title: Spicy Salmon Maki — Singapore HPB Foodo Database (Food Composition)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95 with review_status 'reviewed'. All seven core nutrient fields (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary_fibre) are sourced directly from HPB Foodo. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for spicy salmon roll (FDC ID 2097024) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown; this adaptation is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for spicy salmon maki as a composite dish. GI of 55 is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate component: white sushi rice (short-grain, vinegar-seasoned), which has a published GI of approximately 55 (medium) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (55) × carbohydrate per serving (45.2 g for 150 g serving) / 100 = 24.9. This is a high GL, consistent with the high-carbohydrate rice base.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 432 mg/100 g with low potassium offset (~120 mg/100 g); a 150 g serving delivers approximately 648 mg sodium, representing 28% of the 2300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit spicy salmon maki to no more than 6 pieces per sitting, avoid additional soy sauce dipping (which adds ~900 mg sodium per 15 ml), and request reduced salt in rice seasoning to keep cumulative sodium within safe daily targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 30.1 g/100 g from high-GI white sushi rice (GI ~55) with added sugar in rice seasoning; a 150 g serving delivers ~45 g carbohydrate and a glycemic load of ~25, which is high.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should restrict spicy salmon maki to a half-roll (3–4 pieces) per meal, pair with non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glycemic response, and request brown rice substitution where available to reduce the glycemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Salmon provides cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA); however, sodium at 432 mg/100 g and spicy mayonnaise contribute saturated fat (~1.5 g/100 g) and caloric density that require portion control.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular health, spicy salmon maki can be consumed in moderation (one roll, 150 g) as the omega-3 content from salmon is beneficial, but individuals should avoid soy sauce dipping and request reduced mayo to minimise sodium and saturated fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Salmon is a primary source of anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA); saturated fat is low at ~1.5 g/100 g, supporting EULAR dietary guidance for RA.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Spicy salmon maki is generally suitable for rheumatoid arthritis due to the salmon omega-3 content; however, individuals sensitive to nightshades should request omission of sriracha/chili from the spicy mayo, and portion should be limited to reduce refined carbohydrate intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spicy salmon maki is a Japanese-style sushi roll (makizushi) consisting of seasoned short-grain white sushi rice and raw salmon pieces coated in a spicy mayonnaise dressing, tightly rolled in a sheet of dried nori seaweed and sliced into 6–8 cylindrical pieces. The rice is seasoned with rice vinegar, sugar, and salt, giving it a mildly tangy and slightly sweet flavour. The spicy mayo — typically a blend of Japanese mayonnaise and sriracha or chili sauce — imparts a creamy, pungent heat that contrasts with the rich, buttery texture of the raw salmon. Per 100 g, the roll provides 204 kcal, 4.6 g protein, 7.98 g fat, and 30.1 g carbohydrate, with 432 mg sodium and 2.45 g dietary fibre. The dominant carbohydrate source is white sushi rice, which carries a glycemic index of approximately 55 (medium), yielding a high glycemic load of ~25 per standard 150 g serving. Clinically, the dish is unsuitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes or hypertension in unrestricted portions due to its high-GI carbohydrate load and elevated sodium content. However, the salmon component provides meaningful omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) that are cardioprotective and anti-inflammatory, making it a conditionally suitable choice for cardiovascular health and rheumatoid arthritis when portion-controlled and prepared with reduced sodium. The dish is widely consumed in Singapore, Japan, and globally as a restaurant and takeaway sushi item.

Can I eat spicy salmon maki with rheumatoid arthritis?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95 with review_status 'reviewed' and provenance 'canonical'. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, cholesterol, and potassium are adapted from USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 2097024, spicy salmon roll) and documented. GI is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate component (white sushi rice) using the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008, which is the closest defensible approved-source analogue.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.5 g/100 g), monounsaturated fat (2.1 g/100 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (3.8 g/100 g), cholesterol (18 mg/100 g), and potassium (120 mg/100 g) are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entry for spicy salmon roll (FDC ID 2097024) as HPB Foodo does not report these sub-fractions. Values are plausible and consistent with the salmon and mayonnaise composition of the dish. 2. calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific spicy salmon maki preparation are not available in the HPB Foodo database, and the USDA FoodData Central entry for this composite dish does not report all micronutrient fractions with sufficient specificity for a composite sushi roll. 3. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed negligible/trace per USDA FoodData Central for this food type). 4. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on white sushi rice as the dominant carbohydrate source; no direct GI measurement for spicy salmon maki as a composite dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. 5. Halal status is marked false as conventional spicy mayo typically contains non-halal-certified ingredients; halal-certified versions may be available from specific vendors. 6. The input health_suitability entries for diabetes, hypertension, and rheumatoid_arthritis have been reviewed and incorporated into health_context with updated condition naming (type2_diabetes) and clinical guidance format per Veritas v2 requirements.
